lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market civic and social organizations that repeatedly serve unaffiliated members or participants through dues-supported membership, clubs, alumni or ethnic associations, scouting and youth programs, fraternal or veterans' organizations, recreation, events, facilities, and related food or beverage services when there is a durable external-member service model.
lens.excluded_activities: Social advocacy organizations, business and professional associations, labor and political organizations, religious organizations, commercial gyms or recreation facilities classified elsewhere, captive employer or school units, residential fraternity and sorority houses, insurance offices of fraternal benefit organizations, volunteer-only groups, shells, and organizations without repeat external-member or participant revenue.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers and beneficiaries are members, families, alumni, veterans, ethnic or fraternal constituencies, youth participants, and event or facility users. Revenue commonly combines recurring dues and initiation fees with program charges, events, food and beverage sales, facility use, contributions, sponsorships, and grants.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment and wages by occupation for four-digit NAICS 813400, which corresponds to 813410 but does not measure wage-weighted task exposure. I map member and participant records, billing, renewals, scheduling, event planning support, communications, marketing, bookkeeping, reporting, inventory forecasting, and routine administration as exposed, while retaining childcare, recreation leadership, instruction, food preparation and service, safety, facility work, conflict resolution, member relationships, governance, and accountable event operations as human. Because management, business, and office roles are higher paid than many direct-service roles, a wage weighting raises exposure above a simple job-count mapping; however, the exceptionally large recreation, food-service, childcare, protective, and facility workforce limits the exposed share.
a.rationale: BLS shows that personal care and service and food preparation and serving alone account for nearly half of broad industry employment, while office support, management, and business operations form a smaller but higher-paid administrative layer. O*NET recreation evidence confirms that scheduling, records, reporting, and planning coexist with in-person leadership, safety, first aid, facilities, and participant service.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task survey across clubs, scouting and youth groups, fraternal and veterans' organizations, alumni associations, and other member organizations separating administrative, food, recreation, childcare, facility, safety, and governance hours would materially change the range.
caveats: OEWS reports occupation headcount and wages, not task exposure or payroll-weighted task hours.
The code mixes member administration with childcare, recreation, fitness, bars, restaurants, events, and facilities, producing sharply different exposure by organization.
O*NET recreation occupations illuminate a large service line but do not cover all membership and hospitality work.
The injected compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and may be distorted by nonprofit accounting, volunteers, and contributed facilities.

rho.rationale: Member onboarding, renewals, communications, event scheduling, routine inquiries, bookkeeping support, reporting, marketing drafts, volunteer coordination, and inventory or staffing forecasts can be embedded in association and club systems within five years. Adoption is constrained by small technology budgets, old membership systems, fragmented local chapters, privacy involving children and members, volunteer governance, seasonal staffing, payment and alcohol controls, safety obligations, and the need for people in physical service delivery.
rho.change_evidence: Controlled deployments across representative 813410 service lines measuring adoption, administrative time, renewal and participation effects, schedule and billing errors, safety incidents, staff acceptance, integration cost, and recurring software expense would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No source measures five-year implementation of exposed work in 813410.
National organizations with modern member platforms and independent local clubs face very different implementation constraints.
Automation of administration does not remove the staffing needed for food, recreation, childcare, events, and facilities.

e.rationale: Recurring dues, initiation fees, programs, events, food and beverage, and facility use create recognizable repeat external-member service economics for a meaningful subset. Eligibility is reduced by volunteer-led groups, donor-dependent nonprofits, chapters controlled by national bodies, entities with restricted assets, clubs centered on nontransferable member relationships or real estate, very seasonal programs, and organizations without professional systems or normalized transferable cash flow.
e.change_evidence: A matched Business Register, IRS Form 990, membership-management, liquor-license, facility, and transaction sample classifying organizations by legal form, paid membership, earned-revenue mix, chapter control, normalized cash flow, asset restrictions, and contract or membership transferability would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The NAICS definition establishes a membership-service population but not the share with transferable lower-middle-market economics.
Nonprofit and fraternal legal forms can restrict ownership, assets, membership rights, and use of proceeds.
The injected n of 314 is margin-bridged using a for-profit business-and-consumer-services margin that may not fit clubs, youth organizations, or volunteer-supported nonprofits.

s5.rationale: Many civic and social organizations have no saleable equity owner, and executive succession, board turnover, chapter reorganization, or dissolution is not a qualifying control transfer. A limited subset of for-profit clubs, service operations, facilities, or transferable program businesses can sell, while nonprofit entities can occasionally merge or transfer assets and contracts, but qualifying third-party control events should remain uncommon over five years.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year cohort from state charity and corporate registries, IRS dispositions, liquor and facility license transfers, nonprofit merger records, chapter affiliations, and private deal databases identifying qualifying control or asset transfers among eligible 813410 organizations would materially change the probability.
caveats: No cited source measures qualifying control transfers in this industry.
Board succession, executive replacement, chapter consolidation, and dissolution do not necessarily transfer a durable business to an acquirer.
Member approval, national charters, restricted assets, licenses, and facility ownership can block or reshape a transaction.

q.rationale: Annual dues, initiation fees, fixed program charges, event fees, and existing food or facility prices can preserve implemented savings during a membership term. Retention erodes at renewal through member price sensitivity, competitive clubs and recreation options, nonprofit budgeting, service expectations, wage and food inflation, software cost, and reinvestment in programs, staffing, safety, or facilities rather than margin.
q.change_evidence: Organization-level cohorts showing dues and fee schedules, renewal repricing, churn, program and food contribution margins, member utilization, restricted versus unrestricted revenue, software and payment costs, and realized savings after automation would materially change retention.
caveats: No source measures retention of implemented AI benefits in 813410.
Dues, program fees, food and beverage, events, donations, and grants have different pass-through dynamics.
For nonprofits, retained savings may become service capacity rather than distributable owner value.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment in broad NAICS 813400 Civic and Social Organizations from 364,400 in 2024 to 375,700 in 2034, or 0.3% annually, and constant-dollar output for combined NAICS 8134-9 civic, social, professional, and similar organizations from $115.6 billion to $141.5 billion, or 2.0% annually. Neither directly measures five-year constant-price demand for the current 813410 service basket. I anchor between five years of the narrow employment trend and the broader output trend, then widen for membership aging, youth participation, local demographics, discretionary spending, facility economics, and social substitution. The combined output series may overstate 813410 if professional or business organizations grow faster, while employment may understate quantity if productivity rises.
d5.rationale: Member affinity, youth development, recreation, alumni ties, fraternal networks, veterans' communities, events, and shared facilities remain durable needs, but membership and discretionary participation face demographic change and competition from commercial and online alternatives. Broad projections suggest modest rather than rapid underlying growth.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price 813410 cohorts tracking paid members, renewals, program enrollments, event attendance, facility use, food and beverage volume, chapter openings and closures, and revenue by organization type would materially change the ratio.
caveats: The employment projection is for 813400, while output is available only for the much broader combined 8134-9 population.
Employment and revenue output are imperfect measures of constant-quality member-service quantity.
Demographics, local facility costs, and discretionary spending can move different organization types in opposite directions.

o.rationale: Online communities, self-service scheduling, digital content, remote events, commercial marketplaces, and direct coordination can eliminate some administrative and basic convening quantity. Most of the measured service basket still requires an accountable organization to govern membership, supervise children and recreation, operate facilities and events, serve food and alcohol, manage safety, hold licenses and insurance, resolve conflicts, and preserve a trusted community identity.
o.change_evidence: Data on paid-member migration to online communities, virtual versus in-person participation, software-only club formation, facility and chapter closures, program procurement, insurer and licensing requirements, and substitution toward commercial recreation would materially change this share.
caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share across this heterogeneous industry.
Digital substitution is more plausible for communications and basic affinity groups than for childcare, recreation, food service, facilities, and live events.
An operator may remain necessary even as membership administration becomes largely self-service.

narrative.executive_view: Civic and social organizations combine recurring membership administration with highly physical recreation, childcare, food, events, and facility operations. AI can reduce administrative effort, but most service delivery remains human and site dependent, and nonprofit or fraternal legal forms sharply constrain conventional control acquisitions.
narrative.ai_impact: Useful applications include membership onboarding and renewals, scheduling, routine inquiries, program and event communications, marketing drafts, bookkeeping support, volunteer coordination, reporting, inventory forecasts, and knowledge retrieval. Humans remain essential for recreation leadership, childcare, food preparation and service, safety, facilities, member relationships, conflict resolution, governance, and accountable event execution.
narrative.value_capture: Dues and fixed program or event fees can preserve savings within a membership cycle. Renewal pricing, member expectations, competing leisure options, nonprofit budgets, and reinvestment in programs, safety, staff, or facilities reduce the portion that becomes durable margin.
narrative.firm_availability: A meaningful subset has recurring external-member revenue and professional operations, but the injected stock is margin-bridged using for-profit economics. Volunteer dependence, national chapter control, restricted assets, facility concentration, and the absence of saleable equity reduce the qualifying population and transfer rate.
narrative.demand_durability: Affinity, youth development, recreation, alumni, fraternal and veterans' ties, and in-person community create persistent demand. Growth is likely modest and varies with demographics, discretionary spending, local facility costs, and competition from commercial recreation and online communities.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are nonprofit and fraternal control restrictions, member approval, national charters, facility and real-estate dependence, liquor and childcare regulation, safety and abuse liability, volunteer governance, seasonal staffing, food and wage inflation, local concentration, demographic aging, member churn, cyber and payment risk, and a margin-bridged firm count that may not fit the sector.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring member administration, scheduling, communications, finance, and event-support workflows offer practical AI assistance across many organizations.
narrative.principal_weakness: Physical service delivery and nonprofit or member-controlled legal forms limit both labor substitution and the availability of qualifying control transfers.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Sector 81 Definitions: 813410 Civic and Social Organizations
sources.supports: Defines civic and social organizations as promoting members' civic and social interests, notes that they may operate bars and restaurants, lists major organization types, and identifies adjacent-code boundaries.

sources.title: BLS May 2023 Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 813400 Civic and Social Organizations
sources.supports: Reports 330,770 jobs, including 25.66% personal care and service, 20.54% food preparation and serving, 13.67% office support, 7.68% management, and 5.40% business and financial operations occupations.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Recreation Workers
sources.supports: Describes in-person recreation leadership, safety, first aid, participant needs, facility operations, events, staff supervision, records, attendance, and inventory tasks.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Entertainment and Recreation Managers, Except Gambling
sources.supports: Describes program and event planning, budgets, transaction reconciliation, patron information, policies, scheduling, facilities, staff, complaints, and strong face-to-face responsibility.

sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects NAICS 813400 employment from 364,400 in 2024 to 375,700 in 2034, a 0.3% annual rate, and combined NAICS 8134-9 constant-dollar output from $115.6 billion to $141.5 billion, a 2.0% annual rate.
